blocks.body: Reported Speech helps you pass on statements, questions, commands, and requests without quoting the speaker directly.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"In this section you will see how reported speech works with statements, questions, commands, and requests, and which changes often happen inside the sentence.","rules":[{"label":"Statements","color":"emerald","text":"Start with reported statements: how to pass on a fact, opinion, or piece of information after say or tell.","example":"He said that he was tired."},{"label":"Questions and commands","color":"blue","text":"Then move to reported questions and patterns such as tell / ask + object + infinitive.","example":"She asked if I was ready. / He told me to wait."},{"label":"Changes","color":"amber","text":"Pay special attention to backshift, pronoun changes, and words of time and place.","example":"now → then / here → there / will → would"}]}

blocks.heading: 1. What this section covers
blocks.body:
The first Reported Speech branch stays flat and practical: one top-level category and six core lessons in a learner-friendly order.

  1. Reported Statements - how to report facts and opinions.
  2. Reported Questions - yes/no questions, wh-questions, and word order.
  3. Reported Commands and Requests - tell, ask, order, advise + object + infinitive.
  4. Backshift of Tenses - the most common tense changes after a past reporting verb.
  5. Time and Place Changes - now, today, tomorrow, here, this, these.
  6. Say / Tell / Ask - the difference between three key reporting verbs.

blocks.heading: 2. What to watch for
blocks.body:
• Not every change is automatic: it depends on the reporting verb and the meaning.
  • In reported questions we do not keep question inversion such as did you go; the word order becomes declarative.
  • In reported commands and requests, the pattern is often object + to-infinitive or object + not to-infinitive.
  • Say, tell, and ask are not interchangeable because they control different structures.
